title: Down 55% – but not out
author: Allan O'Neil
topic: 2021 – 12th March – Fire at Torrens Substation, Barker trip and Price Spike
classified_content: The summary of the news "Down 55% – but not out" posted at 15-03-2021 03:47:00 PM is: On March 12, 2021, South Australia's electricity supply faced a significant challenge when a fire in the Torrens Island high voltage switchyard resulted in the loss of approximately 670 MW of generating capacity at AGL's Barker Inlet and Torrens Island B stations. This incident also coincided with around 870 MW of slow-start gas-fired generation and 330 MW from fast-start gas and diesel plants being unavailable, while transmission works in Victoria further limited supply. As a result, electricity spot prices surged dramatically. The incident underscored several key themes in electricity reliability and resilience, including the need for better planning around variable renewable energy outputs and the inherent limitations of transmission systems. The supply situation was eventually stabilized through efforts such as the second gas turbine at Pelican Point being reactivated. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate; prices spiked between 18:00 and midnight on March 12, 2021. Its root cause is: Equipment failure at a switchyard leading to substantial generation capacity loss. Its accident scale is: Region-wide, affecting a significant portion of South Australia's supply capacity. Its dates are: March 12, 2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Major electricity consumers and grid operators in South Australia. Its cause type is: Technical failure and transmission limitations. Its causes are: Fire at Torrens Island switchyard, unavailable generation capacity, transmission works in Victoria, and low renewable energy output due to calm weather and time of day.

title: Preliminary information on loss of SCADA data – for Case Study 24th January 2021 in Queensland
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Preliminary information on loss of SCADA data – for Case Study 24th January 2021 in Queensland" posted at 15-03-2021 11:16:00 AM is: On January 24, 2021, Queensland experienced a total failure in the Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system, impacting Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) operations and causing a price spike to $15,000/MWh due to the rapid reduction in the output of many generators. This incident highlighted the vulnerabilities present in the energy distribution system when reliant on SCADA data. The immediate consequence was a significant constraint on the 'Q>>NIL_CLWU_RGLC' equation, leading to a sudden rise in electricity prices. SCADA services were restored by 16:56, with further investigations ongoing to identify the root cause and implications of the failure. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and short-term during the event on January 24, 2021. Its root_cause is: SCADA system failure. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-01-2021 (Event date) and 15-02-2021 (Preliminary Report date). Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Transmission Network Service Providers (TNSPs) and generating units under Automatic Generation Control (AGC). Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: SCADA system failure, rapid constraint application, generator output reduction, and subsequent pricing spike.

title: Repair work on Heywood continues to affect South Australian region
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2020 – 31st January – Islanding of South Australia after transmission towers destroyed
classified_content: The summary of the news "Repair work on Heywood continues to affect South Australian region" posted at 14-03-2021 02:15:00 PM is: The ongoing repair work on the Heywood interconnector has caused significant volatility in the South Australian electricity market, evidenced by sharp fluctuations in electricity prices and a current cumulative price of $65,000/MWh. A Market Notice by AEMO highlighted a possible intervention to ensure power system security due to insufficient forecast demand, with a potential need for curtailing non-scheduled wind and distributed PV to maintain operational stability. The market has experienced a period of negative electricity prices due to these repairs. Various factors may contribute to changes in demand forecasts, including cloud cover affecting solar output and market responses from Distributed Energy Resources. Despite these challenges, some gas and wind operations have continued, indicating potential compensation for losses incurred during operation. The ongoing nature of the repairs affects market dynamics and highlights supply infrastructure vulnerabilities. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing as of 14/03/2021, particularly affecting afternoon demand around scheduled repair and curtailment periods. Its root cause is: Ongoing repair work on the Heywood interconnector causing system instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 14-03-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Local electricity consumers and grid operators. Its cause type is: Infrastructure repair and maintenance issue leading to market disruptions. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Repair work on Heywood interconnector, forecast demand shortfalls, possible cloud cover affecting solar, potential curtailment of non-scheduled wind and PV, and market reactions to negative pricing.

title: $15,000/MWh in South Australia on Friday 12th March 2021 … part 2 (what seems to have happened)
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2021 – 12th March – Fire at Torrens Substation, Barker trip and Price Spike
classified_content: The summary of the news "$15,000/MWh in South Australia on Friday 12th March 2021 … part 2 (what seems to have happened)" posted at 13-03-2021 01:20:00 AM is: The report details the events of March 12th, 2021, when electricity prices in South Australia spiked to the market cap of $15,000/MWh due to a combination of factors. Key among these was a fire at the Torrens Island substation, which caused the Barker Inlet peaking plant to trip, thereby decreasing available generation. Additionally, repairs on the Heywood interconnector limited imports from Victoria. Simultaneously, wind generation was notably low, and solar generation dropped as expected with the setting sun, exacerbating the tight supply-demand balance. Certain gas and liquid plants didn't operate at full capacity, and South Australia's three large batteries showed a decrease in output, potentially due to low charge levels. Constraints on interconnector flows further impacted the region's electricity supply.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with continued high prices throughout the evening of March 12, 2021. Its root_cause is: Fire at Torrens Island substation, resulting in the trip of the Barker Inlet peaking plant. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12-03-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Infrastructure failure and generation shortfall. Its causes are: Fire at Torrens Island substation, trip of Barker Inlet plant, forecast decline in wind and solar generation, constraints on Heywood interconnector, limited output from gas and liquid peaking plants, low battery charge, and grid constraints.

title: 100% Renewables – A year in practice
author: Adam Clarke
classified_content: The summary of the news "100% Renewables – A year in practice" posted at 11-03-2021 02:58:00 PM is: The City of Newcastle (CN) transitioned to a 100% renewable electricity supply for all its operations in 2020, becoming one of the first local governments in Australia to do so. The switch involved purchasing electricity through a Retail Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) from the Sapphire Wind Farm, alongside implementing an Energy Supply Agreement from the spot market. Despite a critical first test that saw the spot market price skyrocket, CN managed to achieve a 12.15% cost reduction against the Business-As-Usual (BAU) projections over the year, while still providing 100% renewable electricity. By matching renewable energy generation with CN's electricity load profile, the city managed to hedge against spot market price volatility successfully. A significant component of this strategy was the ongoing installation of solar PV across sites and focusing on alternative renewable energy generation like wind for high evening demands. The initiative also included steps to firm renewable supply and build electrical resilience, like upgrading street lighting and installing battery storage. Looking forward, CN plans to enhance demand-side management, upgrade to smart lighting, expand renewable infrastructure, and utilize emerging technologies for further optimizations. In summary, the year-long data showcased the viability and benefits of their approach to renewable energy management, even amid challenges like the COVID-19 pandemic. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: One year (2020). Its root_cause is: Transition to a 100% renewable electricity contract by the City of Newcastle. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-03-2021 02:58:00 PM. Its affected region is: City of Newcastle, Australia. Its affected key users are: City of Newcastle municipal operations. Its cause type is: Energy policy decision. Its causes are: Switch to 100% renewable electricity, Retail power purchase agreement with Sapphire Wind Farm, Implementation of Energy Supply Agreement, Renewable energy management, Solar PV installation, Demand-side management, Battery storage strategy.

title: AEMC publishes Rule Determination on Semi-Scheduled Generators
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMC publishes Rule Determination on Semi-Scheduled Generators" posted at 11-03-2021 12:30:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) has finalized a rule determination stating that semi-scheduled generators must adhere to a megawatt (MW) dispatch level provided in a dispatch instruction for all dispatch intervals. The rule allows variation in response to energy source availability except during a 'semi-dispatch interval' where exceeding the dispatch level is prohibited. This change seeks to address issues where generators previously switched off at negative price points, potentially worsening system inefficiencies.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Starting from 12th April 2021. Its root_cause is: Generator behaviors that affected dispatch outcomes and system efficiencies during negative price conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-03-2021. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled generators within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Policy change. Its causes are: Need to address instances where semi-scheduled generators deviated from dispatch instructions during negative pricing, risking market inefficiency and grid stability.

title: Confidential ‘safety net’ agreement stuck between the Victorian Government and Yallourn Power Station to keep power station operational to mid 2028
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Confidential ‘safety net’ agreement stuck between the Victorian Government and Yallourn Power Station to keep power station operational to mid 2028" posted at 10-03-2021 08:00:00 PM is: This article discusses the arrangement between the Victorian Government and EnergyAustralia to keep the Yallourn Power Station operational until mid-2028. Initially planned to shut down in 2032, the closure has been brought forward, prompting concerns over a potential spike in electricity prices due to the 20% supply loss in Victoria. A $10 million support package for the affected workforce and a confidential “safety net” agreement have been made to prevent an even earlier closure amidst plummeting wholesale power prices caused by cheap renewables. This agreement ensures continuous operation despite negative gross margins. The news was referenced in subsequent articles and reports outlining the broader implications for the energy sector.; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Near to mid-term (until mid-2028). Its root_cause is: The planned early closure of the Yallourn Power Station due to economic challenges and policy changes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10th March 2021. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in Victoria, employees of Yallourn Power Station. Its cause type is: Economic and policy-driven. Its causes are: The influx of cheap renewables causing low wholesale power prices, leading to economic non-viability of the coal-fired station, necessitating government intervention.

The planned outage for the Moorabool-Mortlake 500 kV line is scheduled from March 12 to March 19, 2021, during which there is a risk of synchronous separation of the South Australian region from the NEM, potentially leading to load interruptions. The contingency is managed with specific FCAS sourcing strategies. This follows the severe weather event that caused tower damages requiring a temporary fix and now transitioning to permanent structures. The work requires strict assessment processes to ensure system security. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. title: Next domino? Yallourn closing in 2028 … four years earlier than original plan.
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Next domino? Yallourn closing in 2028 … four years earlier than original plan." posted at 10-03-2021 12:34:00 PM is: EnergyAustralia has announced the closure of the Yallourn coal power plant in mid-2028, which is four years earlier than initially planned. This decision comes amidst the general trend of coal generators in the National Electricity Market (NEM) closing, a reflection of the decreasing profitability of coal as energy shifts towards cleaner alternatives. Additionally, this transition is likely to impact the VIC-NSW interconnector significantly and bring forward the need for stress testing by AEMO in anticipation for future high-demand scenarios, such as the summer of 2028-29, echoing the lessons from past events like the 2019-20 summer. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term, particularly significant from 2028 onwards. Its root_cause is: Policy shift towards cleaner energy and declining profitability of coal power plants. Its accident scale is: N/A (not an accident). Its dates are: 10-03-2021 12:34:00 PM (announcement date), with impacts felt up to and beyond 2028. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers in the region, potentially affecting pricing and stability. Its cause type is: Policy and market-driven. Marcelle's analysis aims to provide clarity on solar farms' economic performance, challenging assumptions embedded in historical business models. title: Lets talk about demand
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "Let's talk about demand" posted at 08-03-2021 02:23:00 PM is: The article evaluates the decline in electricity's share of energy consumption in Australia, opposing the goals of decarbonization, and identifies cooler weather and halted immigration due to COVID-19 as significant factors in reduced electricity demand during the summer of 2021 compared to the previous year. It also highlights the challenges of maintaining and interpreting complex electricity demand models and questions the accuracy of AEMO's behind-the-meter supply estimates. title: Spot price arbitrage with battery storage – a review of 2020 performance
author: Andrew Wilson
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price arbitrage with battery storage – a review of 2020 performance" posted at 03-03-2021 09:19:00 AM is: The University of Queensland (UQ) installed a 1.1 MW / 2.2 MWh Tesla Powerpack battery system at its St Lucia campus in late 2019 to participate in the wholesale electricity spot market. In 2020, UQ's battery, using a custom control system, generated nearly $45,000 in net revenue from energy arbitrage, surpassing predictions by 28% due to favorable price spreads. Revenue peaked in November, with most income obtained during regular price intervals. Key utilization remained below warranty limits, suggesting operational headroom. Ancillary charges remained a cost factor due to roundtrip efficiency losses. The report underscores the potential for refinements in trading strategies to enhance revenue. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2020. Its root_cause is: UQ's strategic participation in the wholesale electricity market with battery storage. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 03-03-2021 09:19:00 AM. Its affected region is: St Lucia campus, Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: University of Queensland and its energy consumers. Its cause type is: Operational strategy in energy market. Its causes are: Implementation of battery storage for energy arbitrage, favorable price spreads, effective control algorithms, and market participation.

title: Report ‘Fast Erosion of Coal Plant Profits’ prompts questions
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Report ‘Fast Erosion of Coal Plant Profits’ prompts questions" posted at 26-02-2021 06:38:00 PM is: The article discusses a report titled "Fast Erosion of Coal Plant Profits in the National Electricity Market" by Green Energy Markets and the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis. It highlights a potential "tidal wave" of renewable solar and wind power developments likely to phase out coal plants sooner than their scheduled retirement dates. Numerous media outlets have covered the report, indicating the increasing financial unviability of coal plants due to renewable energy growth. The article also touches on skepticism about the modeling's credibility while emphasizing the changing landscape of Australia's energy market. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long-term. Its root_cause is: Rapid growth in the development of renewable energy sources like solar and wind. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-02-2021 (release of the report). Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: Coal plant operators and stakeholders in the Australian National Electricity Market. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes are: Increase in renewable energy developments challenging the economic viability of coal power plants.

title: High temperatures drive QLD Scheduled Demand to 9,476MW (highest this summer, so far)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "High temperatures drive QLD Scheduled Demand to 9,476MW (highest this summer, so far)" posted at 22-02-2021 10:01:00 PM is: The surge in Queensland's electricity demand to 9,476MW, the highest recorded for the summer of 2020-21, was primarily driven by high temperatures and reduced solar PV output as evening approached. This temperature-driven demand increase was a notable event considering the La Nina weather conditions, which generally lead to cooler temperatures. During the peak demand period, solar PV contributions drastically dropped, whereas gas generation increased to compensate, leading to a significant spike in electricity prices to $13,989.70/MWh. The event marked only the tenth occurrence of demand exceeding 9,000MW during this summer season. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, affecting the price during the evening on the day of the event. Its root_cause is: High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22-02-2021. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in Queensland. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: High temperature, reduced solar PV output, increased evening electricity demand, increased reliance on gas generation.

title: What does the GSD2020 reveal, about the challenges facing the 12 x AGL coal-fired units
author: Nick Bartels
classified_content: The summary of the news "What does the GSD2020 reveal, about the challenges facing the 12 x AGL coal-fired units" posted at 18-02-2021 11:35:00 AM is: The article analyzes the performance of AGL's coal-fired units using data from the Generator Statistical Digest 2020. The Bayswater and Liddell plants have shown consistent operating ranges over the past two years, with Liddell units experiencing frequent outages and declining capacity factors. Loy Yang A's average loads have decreased, partly due to operational issues and negative spot prices. Liddell and Bayswater units endured multiple outages, impacting their reliability, while Loy Yang A adjusted its operation to cope with market conditions. The analysis points to ongoing challenges with aging infrastructure and market dynamics for AGL. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium term. Its root_cause is: Aging infrastructure and declining market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 18-02-2021 11:35:00 AM. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: AGL Energy and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Operational and economic. Its causes are: Aging coal-fired units, frequent outages, declining capacity factors, and adjustments to market conditions.

title: The accelerating rise of Negative Prices in the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The accelerating rise of Negative Prices in the NEM" posted at 17-02-2021 09:46:00 AM is: The article discusses the increase in negative electricity prices within the National Electricity Market (NEM) throughout 2020, particularly in Queensland, South Australia, Victoria, and Tasmania. It highlights the growing challenge of maintaining profitability for energy producers due to increased solar and wind power generation, leading to periods of negative pricing. In Queensland, increased rooftop PV and coal plant bidding at negative prices during the day are contributing factors. In South Australia and Victoria, excess wind and solar production during early morning and midday respectively are highlighted. This persistent trend is suggested to continue into 2021 and beyond, potentially resulting in unfavorable market dynamics unless significant systemic changes occur. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2020 and potentially accelerating further into 2021 and 2022. Its root_cause is: Increased penetration of renewable energy sources (solar and wind) in the electricity supply mix. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2020, as covered in the analysis. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions including Queensland, South Australia, Victoria, and Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Electricity producers and market participants within the NEM. Its cause type is: Market dynamics driven by increased renewable energy supply and reduced demand during certain periods. Its causes are: Rising adoption of rooftop PV, increased wind and solar generation, reduced grid demand, and negative pricing by coal plants aiming for market flexibility.

title: Reflections (from afar) on the electricity shortages and load shedding in Texas
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Reflections (from afar) on the electricity shortages and load shedding in Texas" posted at 16-02-2021 07:55:00 PM is: The severe winter weather conditions in Texas, and other parts of the USA, caused considerable stress in the electricity sector, leading ERCOT to declare emergency conditions and implement rotating outages due to many generating units going offline. The freezing conditions led to an estimated loss of up to 22,000 MW of generation capacity, necessitating load shedding to balance the grid and prevent further system instability. The extreme weather event not only posed significant challenges to electricity supply but also highlighted the intricacies of grid management amidst growing climate-related risks. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potential future weather events. Its root_cause is: Extreme winter weather conditions. Its accident scale is: Large-scale system disruption. Its dates are: 15-02-2021 (main event date). Its affected region is: Texas and parts of the USA, including the Southwest Power Pool (SPP) and Oregon. Its affected key users are: Approximately two million households and essential services such as water treatment facilities. Its cause type is: External and system-wide. Its causes are: Freezing weather leading to generation loss, grid imbalance, increased demand due to heating needs, and operational challenges for both renewable and fossil fuel-based generators.

title: Low demand experienced in SA on Sunday 14th February 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low demand experienced in SA on Sunday 14th February 2021" posted at 14-02-2021 06:00:00 PM is: On February 14, 2021, South Australia experienced a significant drop in electricity demand due to mild weather conditions and high rooftop PV injections, leading to a record low 'TOTAL DEMAND' in the National Electricity Market (NEM). This scenario resulted in negative electricity prices, impacting the fuel mix with steady gas generation and reduced wind and large solar outputs. Notably, wind production briefly dropped below 100MW, while some Semi-Scheduled plants ramped up briefly coinciding with a price rise. The low demand and negative prices reflect ongoing challenges in grid management amid fluctuating renewable energy contributions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and short-term impact on February 14, 2021. Its root cause is: Mild weather and high rooftop PV penetration. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (as it's not an accident). Its dates are: 14-02-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity market participants and renewable energy generators in South Australia. Its cause type is: Demand decrease due to mild weather and renewable energy generation. Its causes are: Low underlying demand due to mild weather, high rooftop PV generation, reduced wind and large solar output, and negative pricing in the electricity market.

title: Victoria’s snap lockdown drops demand forecast by ~1000MW
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Impact of Coronavirus on the NEM from March 2020
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victoria’s snap lockdown drops demand forecast by ~1000MW" posted at 12-02-2021 04:41:00 PM is: The Victorian government announced a Level 4 lockdown starting from 23:00 on 12th February to 23:00 on 17th February, significantly affecting electricity demand forecasts in the region. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) quickly integrated these changes into their forecast, showing a notable decrease, particularly for the weekdays following the announcement, forecasting a drop of up to 1,134MW on Tuesday. This adjustment reflects the expected impact of reduced economic and social activity due to the lockdown. However, these are preliminary forecasts that require further monitoring and adjustment based on changing circumstances.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, from 15th February to 17th February 2021 during the lockdown period. Its root_cause is: COVID-19-induced lockdown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 12th February 2021 to 17th February 2021. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Policy and economic activity change due to pandemic restrictions. Its causes are: Sudden implementation of a short-term lockdown in Victoria leading to reduced electricity demand forecasts by AEMO.

title: Pain at AGL Energy … part 2
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Pain at AGL Energy … part 2" posted at 12-02-2021 12:32:00 PM is: AGL Energy announced a significant $2.6 billion impairment, largely driven by $1.9 billion attributed to legacy wind farm off-take arrangements. This has led to a reported first-half loss of $2.3 billion and spurred media coverage suggesting AGL may explore a potential business demerger. Additionally, AGL plans to reduce capital expenditures and defer planned outages without expected reliability impacts. Discussions highlight potential impacts on legacy thermal coal assets and their operating patterns, reliability, and potential early closures amidst changing market dynamics like increased renewable PPA agreements. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium term. Its root_cause is: Financial strains due to legacy renewable energy contracts and operational challenges. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates are: 12-02-2021; includes references to events and coverage on 4th February 2021 and preceding weeks. Its affected region is: Primarily Australia. Its affected key users are: AGL shareholders, investors, and potentially large energy consumers. Its cause type is: Financial and operational adjustment. Its causes are: Legacy wind farm off-take arrangements, operational and financial strategies to mitigate losses, media and market reactions, potential demerger considerations.

title: Analysis of Large Thermals using the GSD2020
author: Nick Bartels
classified_content: The summary of the news "Analysis of Large Thermals using the GSD2020" posted at 12-02-2021 08:22:00 AM is: The article examines the operational changes in large thermal power stations in Queensland and South Australia due to the rise of renewable energy generation, highlighting their response to negative spot prices. It notes that fossil fuel plants, notably coal-fired and Combined Cycle Gas Turbines (CCGTs), are adjusting their loading patterns more frequently due to the effects of increasing solar and wind generation, rooftop solar, and lower electricity demand. The ability to cycle, particularly for gas turbines, and maintaining flexibility to attain lower minimum loads, is helping these plants manage financial losses and efficiency under these changing market conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing since 2019. Its root cause is: Rise of renewable energy generation leading to changes in electricity demand and pricing patterns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2019-2020. The affected region is: Queensland and South Australia. The affected key users are: Operators of large coal-fired and gas power stations. The cause type is: Market-driven changes due to renewable energy penetration. The causes are: Increase in large-scale solar and wind generation; rising rooftop solar installations; resultant negative spot prices affecting operational strategies of thermal power stations.

title: AEMO publishes Handbook for Wind and Solar Generators
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO publishes Handbook for Wind and Solar Generators" posted at 11-02-2021 03:38:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released a new guide—‘NEM Operational Forecasting and Dispatch Handbook for wind and solar generators’ to aid semi-scheduled and non-scheduled intermittent generators in understanding and complying with registration and dispatch obligations in the National Electricity Market (NEM). It outlines operational forecasting requirements, integration with AEMO’s forecasting systems, and consequences for inaccurate data inputs. The handbook is part of AEMO's commitment to support variable renewable energy (VRE) generators effectively manage obligations and participate in the market. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term guidance and operational planning. Its root_cause is: Introduction of new operational guidelines for renewable energy generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 11-02-2021. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Wind and solar generators in the NEM. Its cause type is: Administrative/regulatory update. Its causes are: Publication of new handbook and guidelines by AEMO to assist renewable energy generators in the NEM.

title: What’s it like being on a real-time electricity pricing plan? Observations from my poorly insulated townhouse
author: Gordon Leslie
topic: Personal experiences with Distributed Energy Resources
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s it like being on a real-time electricity pricing plan? Observations from my poorly insulated townhouse" posted at 10-02-2021 08:46:00 AM is: The article discusses the author's experience of switching to a real-time retail electricity pricing plan from a traditional flat-rate tariff. The author describes savings achieved under this new plan but highlights the challenges and risks related to volatile spot prices, particularly for households with standard appliances. Attentiveness to pricing through a mobile app has allowed the author to make strategic energy use decisions to optimize cost savings. Challenges such as non-automated appliances and high network charges are also addressed, as well as the importance of hedging to manage price volatility. The article concludes with broader reflections on the structural inefficiencies and potential benefits of real-time pricing for different demographic profiles. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Residential satisfaction. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Transition to a real-time pricing plan by the consumer. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 10-02-2021 08:46:00 AM. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Residential users on real-time pricing plans. Its cause type is: Personal experience with electricity pricing. Its causes are: Transition to a real-time electricity pricing plan, assessment of cost savings and risk, adaptation to usage patterns for cost efficiency.

title: New Sources of Strength
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "New Sources of Strength" posted at 09-02-2021 02:49:00 PM is: The article discusses changes in the management of system strength constraints in South Australia, prompted by AEMO's interventions to maintain grid stability. These interventions often involve directing large synchronous generators to remain online to ensure system strength, as non-synchronous generation does not inherently provide this quality. The installation of synchronous condensers by ElectraNet in South Australia is anticipated to alleviate some of these constraints, potentially reducing the frequency of AEMO interventions and curtailments of non-synchronous generation, thus impacting future grid operations and spot prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short to Medium term, following the commissioning of the synchronous condensers. Its root cause is: The need for maintaining system stability and robustness in South Australia's grid with increased non-synchronous generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 09-02-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Large synchronous and semi-scheduled renewable generators. Its cause type is: System strength management and grid stability. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Increased non-synchronous generation, lack of inherent system strength provision, AEMO interventions, installation of synchronous condensers.

title: Quarterly Renewables Wrap – Q1 2021
author: Ben Willacy
classified_content: The summary of the news "Quarterly Renewables Wrap – Q1 2021" posted at 09-02-2021 01:05:00 PM is: The article discusses the significant progress made in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) during Q4 2020 concerning variable renewable energy (VRE) development, notably in wind and solar capacity. It highlights the ongoing construction boom, with nearly 1 GW breaking ground and 1.34 GW energizing in Q4 2020, illustrating investor confidence and momentum in renewable projects despite delays in commissioning. By the end of 2020, the NEM reached 10 GW of operational utility wind and solar capacity. However, delayed commissioning at large projects like Dundonnell (VIC) and Coopers Gap (QLD) hindered further capacity increase. This article projects that Q1 2021 will be a landmark period for commissioning 1.5 GW of new assets and discusses the long-term forecast, which anticipates the doubling of operational wind and solar capacity to approximately 50% renewables by 2030 due to factors such as state government policies and corporate demand for clean energy.; Its relevance level is: Level 3. Its category is: Energy policy. Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market: Short to medium term with potential for impacts on future electricity prices depending on the completion and integration speed of new projects into the grid. Its root_cause is: The expanding infrastructure, investor confidence, and favorable energy policies driving renewable project commitments and developments. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 09-02-2021 01:05:00 PM. Its affected region is: Primarily New South Wales and Victoria in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Structural and market-driven. Its causes are: Strong investor confidence, state government energy policies, and ongoing construction and commissioning of wind and solar projects within the National Electricity Market (NEM).

title: Which units were most impacted by constraints in 2020?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Which units were most impacted by constraints in 2020?" posted at 05-02-2021 12:50:00 PM is: The article discusses the extensive use of constraint equations by AEMO to manage the dispatch of energy in the NEM, highlighting the varying severity and incidence of these constraints across different generator unit types. It outlines the systemic challenges faced by generators, especially Semi-Scheduled assets, due to network limitations and the impact of system stability requirements such as System Strength, leading to increased hours of being constrained. The analysis in the GSD2020 reflects trends indicating significant increases in constraints over the years, which emphasize the importance of understanding network conditions and the need for transparency in market reporting to avoid sub-optimal decisions in the energy market. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The events affecting electricity markets are discussed in a historic context up to the year 2020, with implications for future market preparedness and investment considerations. Its root_cause is: The increasing incidence of constraints due to network limitations, system strength issues, and expanding non-synchronous generation capacity leading to systemic changes in the dispatch process and market operations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: The article reviews data primarily for the year 2020, with historical trends analyzed over a 10-year period. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Semi-Scheduled and Scheduled Generators, Wholesale Market Participants. Its cause type is: Network and market operational challenges. Its causes are: The increasing complexity of maintaining system strength and accommodating new energy assets within the existing network constraints, compounded by non-synchronous generation integration and evolving market dynamics.

title: AGL Energy announces $2.6b impairment … of which $1.9b for legacy wind farm off-take agreements
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AGL Energy announces $2.6b impairment … of which $1.9b for legacy wind farm off-take agreements" posted at 04-02-2021 11:35:00 AM is: AGL Energy has announced a significant impairment totaling $2.686 billion, primarily driven by a $1.9 billion provision for onerous contracts related to wind farm off-take agreements, which were agreed at higher prices than current market rates. The impairment also includes $1.112 billion for environmental restoration and $532 million for losses in AGL’s generation fleet and gas assets. The impairments are partially offset by a $878 million positive tax effect. The financial write-downs are a result of declining energy prices and highlight the risk of long-term contracting errors in a competitive market, with shareholders, rather than customers, bearing the costs. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Contractual commitments at higher prices than current market conditions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 04-02-2021. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users are: AGL Energy shareholders. Its cause type is: Financial/contractual. Its causes are: Long-term wind off-take agreements entered at high prices, market shifts leading to lower energy prices, no negative price exclusion clauses in contracts.

title: Highlights from the 2020 GSD
author: David Leitch
classified_content: The summary of the news "Highlights from the 2020 GSD" posted at 01-02-2021 02:14:00 PM is: The third edition of the Generator Statistical Digest (GSD2020) by Global Roam and Greenview Strategic Consulting provides comprehensive data for generating units in the National Electricity Market (NEM) for the year 2020. The report highlights various aspects such as FCAS market dominance by batteries, capacity factors, constraints of energy sources like wind and solar, output data, and WVA prices across different energy types. It underscores the significant role of batteries in the current and future FCAS market and notes the varying capacity factors and constraints faced by different energy sources. Additionally, the data reveals disparities in WVA prices, with some gas stations receiving low prices and solar farms demonstrating a wide range of price outcomes. These insights have implications for understanding the dynamics and profitability within the NEM.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root_cause is: Analysis of statistical data from 2020. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2021. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy analysts and market participants. Its cause type is: Report publication. Its causes are: Release of Generator Statistical Digest (GSD) outlining performance metrics of generating units in the NEM.

title: Using the GSD2020 to explore operating patterns across generation types
author: Marcelle Gannon
classified_content: The summary of the news "Using the GSD2020 to explore operating patterns across generation types" posted at 01-02-2021 10:37:00 AM is: The article discusses insights from the Generator Statistical Digest 2020 (GSD2020), which offers a 10-year review of generator performance in the National Electricity Market (NEM) for the calendar year 2020. It highlights the diverse operating patterns and business models of various generator types, such as baseload coal plants, peaking open-cycle gas turbines, hydro plants, wind farms, and solar farms, through visualized data from the GSD2020. The article delves into how these generators differ in terms of capacity, price thresholds, and economic characteristics. It emphasizes the varying economic strategies of baseload and peaking plants, and notes how renewables like wind and solar tend to generate more when prices are low. The discussion also touches on theoretical aspects of electricity market operations. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term (over a decade). Its root_cause is: Examination of historical performance data and operational patterns of NEM generators. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2021 10:37:00 AM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy analysts, electricity market stakeholders. Its cause type is: Analytical study. Its causes are: Compilation and analysis of 10-year generator performance data, visualization of bidding behavior and capacity usage patterns, and examination of various generation types through GSD2020.

title: Energy Transition: it was Full Steam ahead in 2020!
author: Mark Stedwell
classified_content: The summary of the news "Energy Transition: it was Full Steam ahead in 2020!" posted at 01-02-2021 09:50:00 AM is: The article discusses the key highlights from the 2020 Generator Statistical Digest (GSD2020) for the National Electricity Market (NEM), focusing on new generation capacity and battery storage. A total of 3,724 MW new capacity was added, with a mix of wind and solar energy across various regions. Despite the challenges of 2020, these units operated extensively, indicating a dynamic shift towards renewable energy. The article also notes the limited number of batteries, yet highlights their significant revenue during events like the February South Australia island operation. The transition to renewable energy, with an increasing need for grid services, is expected to progress with new storage solutions. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: 2020 onwards. Its root cause is: Expansion and operational assessment of new renewable generation facilities. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market and operational dynamics. Its causes are: 1. Registration of new wind and solar plants. 2. Battery storage's role and performance during grid events. 3. Ongoing energy transition towards renewables.

title: Supply side price drivers in the NEM
author: Ben Willacy
classified_content: The summary of the news "Supply side price drivers in the NEM" posted at 01-02-2021 08:24:00 AM is: The article discusses the key factors influencing electricity prices in the National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia, emphasizing the impact of variable renewable energy (VRE) such as wind and solar on the market. It highlights that in 2020, while the operational capacity of utility-scale VRE surpassed 10 GW, the actual energy output was lower than expected due to factors like economic curtailment and technical constraints. This shortfall in output could lead to higher electricity prices. The article also notes significant involvement of VRE in the price-setting process and the rising importance of storage solutions to address economic and technical challenges, with storage playing a key role in future electricity pricing. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Near term, as economic curtailment and storage challenges persist. Its root_cause is: Lower than expected capacity factors for VRE due to economic curtailment and technical constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2021. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), covering regions of Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia, and Tasmania. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Lower renewable energy production due to lower capacity factors, economic curtailment, technical constraints, and increased role of wind and solar in price setting.

title: Transition Fooled?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Transition Fooled?" posted at 01-02-2021 07:24:00 AM is: The article discusses the role of gas-fired generation as a transitional energy source within Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) in the context of the broader energy transition from coal to renewable sources. Despite being posited as a "transition fuel" to help lower emissions by replacing coal-fired output, gas generation has seen a decline of over 25% since 2015 due to rising gas prices and increased renewable capacity. The expected increase in gas-generated electricity has not materialized, with emissions reductions largely driven by renewables rather than the anticipated role of gas. The article explores the historical and economic factors driving these trends and questions the viability of gas as a transition source going forward.; Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing since the mid-2010s. Its root cause is: Rising gas prices and increased penetration of renewables. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 01-02-2021. Its affected region is: Australia's National Electricity Market. Its affected key users are: Generators, energy investors, and policymakers. Its cause type is: Economic and policy. Its causes are: Rising gas prices in the east coast market, increased renewable capacity leading to reduced demand for gas generation, and lack of emissions pricing favoring coal's cost advantage over gas.

title: The Generator Statistical Digest 2020 (GSD2020) is released
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The Generator Statistical Digest 2020 (GSD2020) is released" posted at 31-01-2021 09:04:00 PM is: The Generator Statistical Digest 2020 (GSD2020) was finalized on 27th January 2021 and early access was provided to pre-order clients on 29th January 2021, with the formal release set for 1st February 2021. This annual publication, developed by Greenview Strategic Consulting and Global-Roam Pty Ltd, provides comprehensive performance data of generating units within the National Electricity Market (NEM) for 2020, offering insights over the past decade. Notable analysts have contributed initial perspectives on the GSD2020's data, enriching the understanding of energy trends and the operational landscape. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Announcements. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Unknown. Its root cause is: Annual data compilation and analysis. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 31-01-2021. Its affected region is: NEM (Australia's National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Industry analysts and energy market participants. Its cause type is: Data and performance insights release. Its causes are: Compilation of extensive statistical data for operational electricity units within the NEM for the calendar year 2020.

title: Prior to the wider release of the GSD2020 … here’s a 10-year trend of bidding behaviour
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Prior to the wider release of the GSD2020 … here’s a 10-year trend of bidding behaviour" posted at 29-01-2021 05:37:00 PM is: The article discusses the impending release of the Generator Statistical Digest 2020 (GSD2020) developed by Global-Roam Pty Ltd and Greenview Strategic Consulting. It highlights notable trends in bidding behavior in the National Electricity Market (NEM) over the past decade, including the impact of the carbon tax, the closure of Hazelwood power station, and the increasing volume of electricity bids priced below $0/MWh. The article mentions that risk levels in the NEM are rising due to declining volumes in specific pricing bands and notes the potential impact of the upcoming Five Minute Settlement. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term implications from historical data trends. Its root_cause is: Analysis of historical bidding behavior and market changes. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-01-2021 05:37:00 PM. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants and analysts. Its cause type is: Market behavior analysis. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Impact of carbon tax, Hazelwood power plant closure, increased low-price bidding, and rising market risk.

title: AEMO releases ‘Quarterly Energy Dynamics’ for Q4 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases ‘Quarterly Energy Dynamics’ for Q4 2020" posted at 29-01-2021 11:24:00 AM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has released its ‘Quarterly Energy Dynamics’ report for Q4 2020, highlighting notable events such as South Australia's achievement of 100% solar production and the lowest prices in Australia, as well as a surge in NSW power prices following an outage. The report is recognized for providing a comprehensive view of market changes, and its release coincides with the upcoming Generator Statistical Digest 2020, which offers detailed performance metrics of individual generators. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Q4 2020. Its root_cause is: Market developments as outlined in the AEMO report. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-01-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia, New South Wales, and Australia in general. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Market dynamics and operational changes. Its causes are: Increased solar generation, power outage in New South Wales, changes in demand dynamics.

title: Average spot prices through 2020 down at levels not seen for many years
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Average spot prices through 2020 down at levels not seen for many years" posted at 29-01-2021 11:20:00 AM is: The article discusses how average spot prices in the National Electricity Market (NEM) were significantly lower in 2020 compared to previous years due to several factors. These factors include the global impact of COVID-19, which reduced oil and gas prices, leading to lower prices for gas-fired generation and consequently electricity prices in the NEM. Additionally, a less stressful and volatile summer in 2020-21 compared to the previous year, along with the increasing influence of wind and solar generation flooding the market and pushing prices down, contributed to this trend. The Australian Energy Market Operator's Quarterly Energy Dynamics report for Q4 2020 also highlights some of these influences on prices. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Fuel prices. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Throughout 2020. Its root_cause is: Decreased fuel prices and increased renewable energy generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-01-2021. Its affected region is: All regions of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and retail brokers. Its cause type is: Economic and market factors. Its causes are: Lower global oil and gas prices due to COVID-19, decreased summer volatility, increased wind and solar generation leading to lower electricity prices.

title: Heavy rain across Victoria squashes Small and Large Solar on Friday 29th January 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Heavy rain across Victoria squashes Small and Large Solar on Friday 29th January 2021" posted at 29-01-2021 10:46:00 AM is: Heavy rainfall in Victoria, Australia, on January 29, 2021, led to increased electricity demand and reduced solar power generation due to cloud cover across the region. This event highlights the dependency of the National Electricity Market (NEM) on weather conditions, impacting solar production. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate. Its root cause is: Heavy rainfall causing cloud cover. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 29-01-2021. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users are: Solar power generators. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: Heavy rain and thick cloud cover leading to reduced solar power generation and increased electricity demand.

title: Initial thoughts on AEMO’s draft Guidelines for Wholesale Demand Response (WDR)
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Initial thoughts on AEMO’s draft Guidelines for Wholesale Demand Response (WDR)" posted at 28-01-2021 02:17:00 PM is: The article discusses the Australian Energy Market Operator's (AEMO) draft guidelines for the Wholesale Demand Response (WDR) mechanism. These guidelines set requirements for WDR Unit classification, telemetry, baseline methodologies, and adjustments to maximum responsive components. The dispatch and bid information will be available daily, but there's a lack of real-time transparency. The guidelines propose regional telemetry thresholds to mitigate off-target response risks, impacting operational forecasts. Final guidelines are expected in March 2021, with WDR starting on October 24, 2021. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From October 24, 2021, onwards. Its root_cause is: Implementation of new guidelines and mechanisms for Wholesale Demand Response. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 28-01-2021. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions in Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and demand response aggregators within the NEM. Its cause type is: Policy and regulatory change. Its causes are: AEMO's initiative to enhance demand-side participation and market transparency through the new WDR mechanism.

title: Cool change arrives in Melbourne early on Monday 25th January 2021, dampening high demand expectations
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cool change arrives in Melbourne early on Monday 25th January 2021, dampening high demand expectations" posted at 25-01-2021 03:55:00 PM is: A forecasted high electricity demand in Melbourne due to extreme heat was significantly reduced after an unexpected early cool change, causing the temperature to drop. This change resulted in much lower operational and scheduled demand than the AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator) initially anticipated, illustrating forecasting challenges in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Meanwhile, demand in South Australia was affected by ongoing bushfires, leading to increased local electricity prices due to constraints on imports and reduced wind and solar production. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term. Its root_cause is: Unexpected early arrival of cool weather. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 25-01-2021. Its affected region is: Melbourne and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Residents and businesses in Melbourne and South Australia. Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes are: Early cool change, reduced temperatures, ongoing bushfires in South Australia, forecast model inaccuracies, lower demand expectations.

title: Sunday 24th Jan 2021 (part 2) – down to the wire (because of bushfire) in South Australia
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sunday 24th Jan 2021 (part 2) – down to the wire (because of bushfire) in South Australia" posted at 24-01-2021 09:51:00 PM is: On January 24, 2021, bushfires in South Australia's Adelaide Hills resulted in several transmission line trips impacting the energy market. This non-credible contingency caused demand and supply imbalances, leading to price fluctuations. Despite reduced electricity reserves, South Australia managed to avoid load shedding, though high prices persisted due to constrained imports from Victoria. The lack of reserve condition was canceled later that evening. AEMO continued to issue notices to manage system security into the night; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (lasting until January 25, 2021). Its root_cause is: Bushfire-induced transmission line trips. Its accident scale is: Regional. Its dates are: 24-01-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy consumers and market participants in South Australia. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: Bushfire in the Adelaide Hills leading to grid disruptions and electricity price volatility.

title: Sunday 24th Jan 2021 – a couple discrete incidents
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sunday 24th Jan 2021 – a couple discrete incidents" posted at 24-01-2021 06:42:00 PM is: On January 24, 2021, the electricity market experienced several incidents impacting the NEM, including a SCADA failure reported by AEMO and significant price spikes in Queensland and South Australia. The SCADA system failure at 16:33 affected all regions, with Queensland seeing a spike in energy prices at 16:35 and 16:40 due to a constraint affecting generator outputs, likely exacerbated by the SCADA data loss. Concurrently, in South Australia, hot weather drove demand higher, causing volatility and an Actual LOR1 condition. Prices peaked at $5,541.61/MWh at 17:30. Continuing vigilance is essential to navigate these dynamic conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate to short-term. Its root_cause is: SCADA system failure and high demand due to hot weather. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 24-01-2021. Its affected region is: Queensland and South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in QLD and SA regions. Its cause type is: System failure and weather-related demand spike. Its causes are: SCADA failure, constraint on generator outputs, high temperatures leading to increased demand.

title: High temperatures, and high demand, forecast for Monday 25th January 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "High temperatures, and high demand, forecast for Monday 25th January 2021" posted at 22-01-2021 03:29:00 PM is: The Australian Electricity Market Operator (AEMO) has issued warnings for potential high temperatures from 22nd to 26th January 2021, which are expected to impact electricity demand and generation capability across South Australia, Tasmania, and Victoria. Forecasts indicate escalating electricity demand in these regions due to the intense heatwave, while some generating units may reduce capability due to overheating. The high temperatures may result in increased electricity prices and demand challenges, with Victoria forecasting peak demand nearing historical highs. High temperatures can also reduce available generation capacity, especially affecting coal, gas, wind, and solar power plants. AEMO has advised market participants to review their capacity availability to adjust to these forecasts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Short-term, covering 22nd to 26th January 2021. Its root cause is: Prediction of extreme weather conditions, specifically high temperatures. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 22nd to 26th January 2021. Its affected region is: South Australia, Tasmania, and Victoria. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers and market participants in the affected regions. Its cause type is: Environmental. Its causes are: High temperature forecasts exceeding NEM Local Temperature Alert Levels, leading to potential increase in electricity demand and decreased generation capacity.

title: Yesterday’s unexpected loss of ~500MW of rooftop PV in South Australia (another type of Contingency event – Credible, or Non-Credible?)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yesterday’s unexpected loss of ~500MW of rooftop PV in South Australia (another type of Contingency event – Credible, or Non-Credible?)" posted at 14-01-2021 04:38:00 PM is: The article examines the sudden loss of approximately 500MW from rooftop photovoltaic (PV) systems in South Australia on January 13, 2021, which led to a significant spike in operational demand and subsequent electricity prices in the region. This incident was attributed to an unexpected drop in rooftop PV generation due to weather conditions, causing grid demand to increase sharply. This event highlights the challenges faced by grid operators like the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) in managing sudden fluctuations in demand due to the intermittent nature of rooftop solar power. AEMO's response to the coincident trip of Callide C units is discussed, along with its impact on market operations and contingency classifications. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. The timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Immediate and short-term. Its root cause is: Unexpected drop in rooftop PV generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-01-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in South Australia. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage. Its causes are: Rapid decrease in rooftop PV generation, resulting in increased grid demand and price spikes.

title: NEM Local Temperature alert for Dalby (QLD) for Sat 16th Jan 2021 … an opportunity to check the draft GSD2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM Local Temperature alert for Dalby (QLD) for Sat 16th Jan 2021" posted at 13-01-2021 01:05:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a local temperature alert for Dalby, Queensland, due to forecasted high temperatures on January 16, 2021. The alert reminds electricity market participants to review weather forecasts and update their capacity availability offers accordingly. This type of alert can affect the performance of various energy assets, including coal-fired power stations, gas turbines, solar farms, and wind farms, in regions like Dalby due to heightened temperature sensitivity. High temperatures are significant for energy supply chain components, and operators are advised to notify AEMO of any temperature-induced changes in their operations. The event also ties into the ongoing review of the Generator Statistical Digest 2020. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and potentially ongoing during the high temperature period. Its root_cause is: High forecasted temperatures impacting energy assets in the region. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13 Jan 2021 to 17 Jan 2021. Its affected region is: Dalby, Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users are: Energy market participants, specifically those operating in and around Dalby. Its cause type is: Natural. Its causes are: High temperatures in Dalby affecting the operation of local energy assets and necessitating capacity reviews by market participants.

title: Both units trip at Callide C on Wednesday 13th January 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Both units trip at Callide C on Wednesday 13th January 2021" posted at 13-01-2021 11:30:00 AM is: The article reports a rare event where both units at Callide C power station in Queensland tripped simultaneously at 10:57 AM on 13th January 2021, resulting in the loss of approximately 830MW of supply and a subsequent impact on grid frequency and electricity prices. AEMO published a market notice regarding this non-credible contingency event but did not necessitate load shedding. The root cause of the trip remains unknown. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediately following the trip at 10:57 AM, with potential short-term implications due to uncertainty in supply. Its root_cause is: Unknown as per the current status of information. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 13-01-2021. Its affected region is: Queensland. Its affected key users are: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: Simultaneous tripping of both Callide C units.

title: Maximising the benefit of renewables with an intelligent energy management system
author: Robert May
classified_content: The summary of the news "Maximising the benefit of renewables with an intelligent energy management system" posted on 05-01-2021 02:07:00 PM is: The article discusses SA Water's Zero Cost Energy Future program, which aims to optimize energy management for its operations using a combination of solar PV, battery storage, and demand response models. By implementing an intelligent energy management system, SA Water is enhancing operational efficiency and minimizing electricity costs through real-time optimization, data analytics, and AI forecasting. This sophisticated approach allows co-optimization of solar and battery storage with existing generation assets and flexible loads from water and wastewater treatment, ensuring responsiveness to volatile energy prices. The program includes developing decision-support systems and an energy dashboard across operational sites to facilitate monitoring and optimization. The initiative represents a significant step in leveraging renewables and advanced energy management strategies in utility operations. Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Ongoing. Its root cause is: Advancement in energy management and renewable integration. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 05-01-2021. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users are: SA Water and associated operational facilities. Its cause type is: Implementation of energy management systems. Its causes are: Integration of solar PV, battery storage, demand response models, AI forecasting, and real-time optimization for energy management in utility operations.

title: Cranking the handle, to produce the Generator Statistical Digest 2020
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Cranking the handle, to produce the Generator Statistical Digest 2020" posted at 05-01-2021 11:10:00 AM is: The news discusses the upcoming release of the Generator Statistical Digest 2020, which offers a detailed analysis of the technical and commercial performance of supply-side assets in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The report is anticipated to be a comprehensive resource for understanding the health of the NEM, with its release expected on January 27, 2021. The news highlights the importance of this report in identifying weaknesses within the NEM and in taking preemptive actions to prevent critical system failures. Pre-ordering the report offers a cost-saving opportunity for interested clients.; Its relevance level is: Level 3 (Less Relevant). Its category is: Energy policy. title: Status update, on availability of the 48x remaining coal units across Q1 2021
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Status update, on availability of the 48x remaining coal units across Q1 2021" posted at 03-01-2021 02:09:00 PM is: The news article discusses the status of coal-fired units in New South Wales (NSW), Victoria (VIC), and Queensland (QLD) during the first quarter of 2021, highlighting changes in availability due to planned and unplanned outages. In NSW, out of 16 coal units, 15 are generally available, with specific outages at Liddell and Bayswater units. In VIC, 9 to 10 units are available, with future outages at Loy Yang A4 and Yallourn 1. In QLD, out of 22 units, 20 to 21 are operational, affected by outages at Stanwell, Tarong North, and Millmerran units. The update utilizes the new ‘MT PASA DUID Availability’ Widget and indicates improvements in data availability due to rules changes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity market is: Q1 2021. Its root cause is: Outages and planned maintenance of coal units. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: January to March 2021. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Victoria, Queensland. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its cause type is: Combination of planned maintenance and unplanned outages. Its causes are: Liddell unit 3 outage, Loy Yang A3 plant failure, Stanwell unit 2 unit trip, and maintenance activities at various coal-fired units.
